coredata.dc:title: Concentration dependent carbon nanodots: Tunable luminescent color and fluorescence excitation-wavelength dependence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Song, Yang

Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) exhibiting concentration dependent properties have been synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal reaction process utilizing diethylenetriamine and l-aspartic acid. At solid-state or high concentrations, the CNDs display excitation-wavelength independent fluorescence (FL) emissions, while at low concentrations, they exhibit excitation-wavelength dependent FL emissions. Detailed characterization of the structure and optical properties reveals that the concentration dependent FL properties can be ascribed to the intrinsic-state luminescence of the CNDs at low concentrations and the assembled-state luminescence at solid-state/high concentrations. With the increase of concentration, the self-assembly behavior of CNDs may cause the transition from intrinsic-state dominant luminescence to assembled-state dominant luminescence, and finally lead to the red-shift of FL color. Furthermore, the CNDs@Urea composites possess adjustable room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) from turquoise to yellow by controlling the CNDs doping concentration. CNDs with concentration dependent optical properties have shown certain potentials in the fields of cell imaging, fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Concentration dependence
Excitation-wavelength dependence
Excitation-wavelength independence
Room-temperature phosphorescence

The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and concentration-dependent fluorescence characteristics of CNDs.

(a) TEM image of CNDs. (b) Particle size distribution diagrams of CNDs. (c) HRTEM image of CNDs. (d) XRD pattern of CNDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of CNDs. (f) Full scan XPS spectrum of CNDs. (g-i) High resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of CNDs.

Fluorescence spectra of CNDs solutions excited by different wavelength lights in different concentrations. (a) 50.0 mg/mL. (b) 25.0 mg/mL. (c) 12.5 mg/mL. (d) 3.13 mg/mL. (e) 0.781 mg/mL. (f) 0.195 mg/mL. Inset: The fluorescence photographs of CNDs solutions in different concentrations.
Schematic illustration of photoluminescence mechanism of CNDs in different concentrations.

(a) Schematic illustration the structures of CNDs and urea and the formation route of CNDs@Urea powders for room-temperature phosphorescence. (b-c) Photographs of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 composites captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off, respectively. (d-e) The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 powders, respectively. (f-g) The room-temperature phosphorescence decay spectrum of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 powders, respectively.

Fluorescence images of CNDs in HepG2 cells under different conditions. The first line is fluorescence images of HepG2 cells without CNDs in (control group). The second line is fluorescence images of HepG2 cells adding CNDs (200 μg/mL). The third line is fluorescence images of HepG2 cells adding CNDs (25.0 mg/mL). The fourth line is fluorescence images of HepG2 cells adding CNDs (50.0 mg/mL). Excitation-wavelength is 488 nm.

(a) Information encrypted photographs of “BYD” logo made from CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 powders. (b) Photographs of CNDs@Urea-2 and CNDs@Urea-1 powders designing “I Love CZU” and “I Love SEU” patterns captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off, respectively. (c) Photographs of fingerprint of CNDs gel (CNDs powder to CNDs gel, moisture absorption easily) under daylight (left) and 365 nm light (right).

head.ce:title: Concentration dependent carbon nanodots: Tunable luminescent color and fluorescence excitation-wavelength dependence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory Animal Center, Changzhi Medical College, Changzhi 046000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mathematics, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China (J. Bai).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have accomplished concentration-controlled fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence color-changing emitters with excellent luminescence performance.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) exhibiting concentration dependent properties have been synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal reaction process utilizing diethylenetriamine and l-aspartic acid. At solid-state or high concentrations, the CNDs display excitation-wavelength independent fluorescence (FL) emissions, while at low concentrations, they exhibit excitation-wavelength dependent FL emissions. Detailed characterization of the structure and optical properties reveals that the concentration dependent FL properties can be ascribed to the intrinsic-state luminescence of the CNDs at low concentrations and the assembled-state luminescence at solid-state/high concentrations. With the increase of concentration, the self-assembly behavior of CNDs may cause the transition from intrinsic-state dominant luminescence to assembled-state dominant luminescence, and finally lead to the red-shift of FL color. Furthermore, the CNDs@Urea composites possess adjustable room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) from turquoise to yellow by controlling the CNDs doping concentration. CNDs with concentration dependent optical properties have shown certain potentials in the fields of cell imaging, fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs), as a new class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm, have aroused great attention since first discovered in 2004 [1–6]. CNDs are superior to conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) because of their outstanding optical properties, simple preparation, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and ease of surface modification. A large number of CNDs have been synthesized and have shown exciting advantages in the fields of biomedicine [7–11], sensing [12–15], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [16–19], photocatalysis [20,21], anti-counterfeiting [22,23], solar cells [24–26], and so on.

The most fascinating features of CNDs are their tunable fluorescence (FL) and excitation-wavelength dependent/independent properties [27–29]. Many efforts have been made in this field to adjust the luminescence of CNDs and explore the mechanism of luminescence, which is crucial for expanding their diverse applications [30–32]. There are many strategies to achieve modulation of FL emission, such as by regulating the size, surface state, element-doped, or altering the raw materials, reaction temperature and time, and even tuning solvent effect, pH effect, cross-linked enhanced emission (CEE) effect [33,34]. For example, Pang et al. reported CNDs with different FL colors through varying their size by the ultrafiltration method [35]. Zhang et al. reported two types of CNDs exhibiting respective excitation-independent blue emission and excitation-dependent full-color emissions have been synthesized via a mild one-pot process from chloroform and diethylamine [30]. Recently, our group reported three solvent dependent CNDs from o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine by hydrothermal/solvothermal one-step method [16]. The synthesized CDs exhibit polychromatic and typically excitation-wavelength dependent/independent fluorescence in solvents of different polarities. In a variety of different FL regulation methods, tuning the concentration of single CNDs is a straightforward and efficient approach for achieving multicolor FL emission [36]. In typical research studies, Lin et al. reported CNDs that owned concentration-dependent FL properties could be ascribed to the existence of multi-emissive centers of core states, edge states and surface states [37]. Liu et al. synthesized CNDs that owned concentration-dependent FL emissions. Additionally, the solid of CNDs also emitted bright yellow fluorescence under ultraviolet light [34]. However, there are still few comprehensive studies on the FL regulation of the concentration dependence and self-assembly behavior of CNDs [34]. Furthermore, there are even rarer reports about room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) transformation of the same CNDs by concentration regulation in the solid matrix. Hence, it is urgent to develop concentration dependent CNDs with both FL and RTP regulation.

In this study, we report a concentration dependent and color-tunable fluorescent phenomenon in CNDs using common carbon sources of diethylenetriamine and l-aspartic acid via hydrothermal synthesis. The blue to yellow emissions of CNDs can be altered under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp by changing the concentration. Interestingly, the CNDs powders and high concentration solutions exhibit stable FL emissions with different excitation-wavelengths. On the contrary, like the vast majority of previously reported samples, the as-prepared low concentration solutions of CNDs exhibit excitation-dependent FL features. Even more exciting, the CNDs@Urea composites not only have tunable RTP from turquoise to yellow by varying the doping amount, but also show ultralong lifetimes (705 ms for turquoise-RTP and 803 ms for yellow-RTP). Furthermore, the as-prepared CNDs and CNDs@Urea composites can be applied to cellular imaging, fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting applications.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the preparation of concentration dependent CNDs can be easily prepared through a simple hydrothermal process, specifically, heating diethylenetriamine and l-aspartic acid in aqueous solution in autoclaves, and then purified by using dialysis way (see the experimental procedure in support information for details). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman were used to characterize the CNDs to confirm the morphology and structure of carbon nanoparticles. The CNDs’ average diameter is near 4.16 nm, and their size distributions range from 2 to 8 nm (Fig. 2 a and b). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) reveals a lattice spacing measurement of 0.207 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite, indicating that the CNDs encompass a small amount of graphite-like structures (Fig. 2c) [38]. The broad peak around 21° of XRD pattern spectrum in Fig. 2d is attributed to the disordered and amorphous structure [38,39]. The absence of obvious fingerprint peaks of graphene structure at about 1360 cm−1 (d-band) and 1580 cm−1 (G-band) in the Raman spectrum also reveals that the CNDs have a low degree of graphitization (Fig. S1) [40].

To gain further insights into the chemical composition and surface functional groups of CNDs, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were employed, providing detailed spectra for comprehensive evaluation. As illustrated in Fig. 2e, a relatively broad absorption peak emerged at 3275 cm−1, attributable to the stretching vibration modes by NH and OH bonds. The peaks observed at 2927 and 2870 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration mode of CH bonds. The stretching vibration bands of CO and CN were measured around 1765 and 1629 cm−1, while the CC and CN stretching vibration bands were located at 1552 and 1385 cm−1, respectively [28]. The peak detected at 1185 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibration of CO bonds, indicates the significant presence of O-containing functional groups within the structure. Subsequently, the functional groups of CNDs were further confirmed and identified through XPS analysis. The XPS analysis of the CNDs sample presented in Fig. 2f reveals three distinct peaks located at 285, 399 and 531 eV, corresponding to the C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively, indicating that the presence of C, N and O elements in the CNDs. In addition, the atomic ratios are calculated as 64.12 % (C element), 14.27 % (N element) and 21.61 % (O element), respectively (Table S1). As illustrated in Fig. 2g, the C 1 s spectra of CNDs exhibits three prominent peaks located approximately at 284.3, 285.4 and 287.5 eV, which are attributed to the CC/CC, CO/CN and CO/CN bonds, respectively [16,41]. As can be seen from Fig. 2h, the fitted band of N 1s could be divided into C–NC (399.5 eV) and NH (400.5 eV) bonds, respectively [41]. As shown in Fig. 2i, the O 1s could be divided into 530.4, 531.3 and 532.9 eV, which correspond to CO, CO/OH and O–CO bonds, respectively [41]. Therefore, the CNDs are composed of a small number of graphitized structures and surface functional groups like –NH2, –OH and –COOH.

The optical performance of CNDs is evaluated by UV–Vis absorption spectra (Figs. S2a and S3a) and FL spectra (Fig. S4) at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S2a, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of CNDs exhibits two peaks at 208 and 324 nm due to the π-π* transitions [29,38]. While the CNDs powder exhibits a different broad absorption, which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of the surface states containing CN/CO and CO structures (Fig. S3a) [29]. Excitedly, like the other reported samples [28,34,37,42–50], the prepared CNDs exhibits concentration dependent FL properties, enabling the tuning FL emission properties by adjusting its concentration (Fig. S4). The CNDs aqueous solution with concentration of 0.0488, 0.195, 0.781, 3.13, 12.5, 25.0 and 50.0 mg/mL were prepared respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the emission peaks of CNDs shift from 445 to 540 nm under the excitation-wavelength of 360 nm when the CNDs concentration varies from 0.0488 to 50.0 mg/mL.

In order to confirm the luminous mechanism of CNDs, concentration dependent and excitation-wavelength independent/dependent FL spectra are obtained, and the results are shown in Figs. 3 and S2-S5. Both excitation-wavelength independent and dependent CNDs FL phenomenon have been reported by many researchers [27–29,33]; however, this CNDs sample presented totally opposite performance in solid-state/high concentrations and low concentrations, which has been seldom reported. For a series of low concentration aqueous solutions (0.0488–12.5 mg/mL) of CNDs (Fig. 3c-3f and S2b), which exhibit typical excitation-wavelength dependent FL behaviors. For instance, as depicted in Fig. S2b, the FL emission peaks of CNDs in an aqueous solution (0.0488 mg/mL) at different excitation-wavelengths (from 300 to 420 nm) were tested. Obviously, as the excitation-wavelength was varied, the FL emission peaks red-shifted from 396 to 492 nm. In addition, the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectrum of CNDs aqueous solution (0.0488 mg/mL) is explored (Fig. S5b). As can be seen, CNDs exhibits excitation-wavelength dependent luminescence, revealing that they may have different luminescence centers and their luminescence characteristics may dominated by their intrinsic-state [29,50–52]. On the other hand, for solid-state powder and high concentration aqueous solutions (50.0 and 25.0 mg/mL) of CNDs, the FL emission peaks almost remained the same while the excitation signal increased, showing an excitation-wavelength independent characteristic (Fig. 3a, 3b and S3b). For example, Fig. 3a demonstrates that the emission peaks of the CNDs in an aqueous solution (50.0 mg/mL) remained constant at 540 nm, regardless of the excitation-wavelength being adjusted from 340 to 510 nm. Fig. S3b also represents the FL emission peaks of the CNDs powder located at 540 nm under different excitation wavelengths, showing stable yellow emission and excitation-wavelength independent characteristics, that are more similar to traditional inorganic phosphors and organic dyes. Meanwhile, the EEM spectrum of high concentration CNDs aqueous solution (50.0 mg/mL) is also explored (Fig. S5a). Relatively, the high concentration CNDs aqueous solution only has a yellow emission center, indicating that their luminescence characteristics may dominated by assembled-state.

By performing FL analysis and comparing low concentration aqueous solutions and high concentration aqueous/solid-state powder of CNDs, uncommon feature of excitation-wavelength independent/dependent characteristics of CNDs are summarized in Fig. 4 . In general, CNDs synthesized under high temperature and pressure conditions, with significant carbonization, possess multiple FL origins, encompassing both surface-states and core-states [52–54]. This leads to different energy levels, resulting in excitation-wavelength dependent FL characteristics. In dilute solution, the fluorophores exhibit weak interactions due to the distance between the interparticle is sufficiently remote. Therefore, CNDs show excitation-wavelength dependent emission characteristics resulted from the intrinsic-state. Conversely, in concentrated solution or solid-state, the distance becomes shorter increasing the possibility of hydrogen bond formation through interaction among amino and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of CNDs, which leads to changes in the chemical environment on the surface of CNDs, and thus produces new assembled-state emission [36,55].

To investigate the self-assembly behavior of CNDs, both TEM and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterizations were employed [36,56,57]. TEM was utilized to analyze CNDs in aqueous solutions at varying concentrations, while SEM was used to examine CNDs powder. The TEM results showed that as the concentration of CNDs increased, the particle size also grew. Notably, the average particle diameter expanded from 4.69 nm at a concentration of 0.781 mg/mL to 19.88 nm at a concentration of 3.13 mg/mL, exhibiting outstanding dispersion qualities (Fig. S6a–i and Fig. S7). Furthermore, SEM images of CNDs powder disclosed compact assemblies at the micrometer scale, featuring a multilayer stacked morphology (Fig. S6j–l). As shown in Fig. 3e and S8a, when the excitation-wavelength increased from 300 to 420 nm, the emission-wavelength of CNDs (0.781 mg/mL) also red-shifted from 402 to 495 nm (red-shift 93 nm), showing a significant excitation-wavelength dependent FL characteristics. Even more surprising, CNDs (3.13 mg/mL) has shown a lower degree of excitation-wavelength dependent FL (red-shift 54 nm) than CNDs (0.781 mg/mL) under the same excitation-wavelength variation (Fig. 3d and S8b). While the powder formed by the final self-assembly of the CNDs exhibits excitation-wavelength independent FL characteristics (Fig. S3b). Since the network structures are formed by self-assembly of surface functional groups, CNDs shows no excitation-wavelength dependent FL characteristics [58–62]. These observations clearly demonstrate the self-assembly behavior and concentration dependent fluorescence regulation of CNDs.

The CNDs we also utilized to create color-tunable RTP systems. Urea has been chosen as the amorphous rigid environmental matrix due to its abundant amino group as a good hydrogen bond acceptor and donor, which can give effectively suppressed non-radiative deactivation [63,64]. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the –OH, –NH2 and –COOH functional groups present on CNDs are capable of establishing hydrogen bonds with the chosen solid matrix of urea. Thermostability of urea and CNDs@Urea-2 were studied by thermogravimetric (TGA) under N2 atmosphere. As demonstrated in Fig. S9, the urea and CNDs@Urea-2 show mass loss of 51.6 % and 47.6 % at 165–245 ℃, which may be due to the more stable skeleton structure of CNDs@Urea-2, suggesting that hydrogen bonds exist between CNDs and urea [65,66]. The immobilization of CNDs within the urea matrix enhances their ability to emit triplet radiation (RTP). To examine the effect of doping concentration on RTP characteristics, CNDs doped urea with doping concentrations of 0.053 and 3.2 wt% were prepared and their photophysical properties were subsequently analyzed. For instance, the prepared CNDs@Urea-1 powders, featuring a doping concentration of 0.053 wt%, exhibit blue-white luminescence upon UV light (365 nm) and subsequently emit a turquoise phosphorescence once the 365 nm UV illumination terminated (Fig. 5b and Movie 1). While the sample of CNDs@Urea-2 powders, having a higher doping concentration of 3.2 % wt%, displayed green FL upon excitation at 365 nm, and subsequently exhibited yellow phosphorescence once the 365 nm excitation ceased (Fig. 5c and Movie 2).

In order to further understand the phosphorescence properties of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2, the RTP emission and lifetime decay profiles spectra were also measured (Fig. 5d–5g). As shown in Fig. 5d and 5f, the emission centers near 522 and 552 nm corresponding to the long-lived phosphorescence of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 powders. Moreover, the time resolved phosphorescence decay spectrum of CNDs@Urea-1 sample exhibit a tri-exponentially decay lifetime with lifetime components to be 60, 280 and 1032 ms, respectively (Table S2). According to the following equation [16]:
(1)
τ
avg
= Σ α i τ
i 2
/ Σ α i τ i

The average lifetime of the CNDs@Urea-1 was calculated to be 705 ms (Fig. 5f). Similarly, Fig. 5g reveals the afterglow decay spectrum of CNDs@Urea-2 powder, which can be fitted with a tri-exponential function with lifetime components to be 40, 251 and 1125 ms, respectively (Table S2). CNDs@Urea-2 powders show remarkably long lifetime of 803 ms (Fig. 5g). The above-mentioned results indicate that not only the FL emission of solution system can be tuned by changing the concentration of CNDs, but also the concentration dependent color-tunable RTP composite materials can be constructed by adjusting the doping concentration [67–69].

In consideration of concentration dependent FL and RTP luminescence behaviors possessed by CNDs, the potential applications of cell imaging and anti-counterfeiting are investigated. Firstly, since CNDs have concentration dependent FL emissions and relatively low toxicity, we explored its potential applications in cell imaging. The CCK8 assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CNDs in HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. S10, after incubating HepG2 cells with CNDs for 24 h without light, the cell viability remained above 98 % at 120 μg/mL and close to 58 % at 150 μg/mL. Subsequently, a series of CNDs aqueous solutions with different concentrations are incubated with HepG2 cells to explore their FL cell imaging capabilities [70]. When the excitation-wavelength of laser confocal microscope is selected 488 nm, HepG2 cells incubated with high concentration CNDs aqueous solutions can emit bright FL signals (third and fourth lines in Fig. 6 ). On the contrary, HepG2 cells cultured with low concentration of CNDs aqueous solution could not emit obvious FL signal (second line in Fig. 6). This phenomenon is consistent with the CNDs concentration dependent FL phenomenon.

Because of the excellent RTP property of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 compounds, they have a certain potential in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Making use of the distinctive RTP performance of CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2, we carried out time-resolved RTP anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 7 a, CNDs@Urea-1 and CNDs@Urea-2 were loaded into the letter patterns of “BYD”. The patterned security feature of “Y” was filled with phosphorescent CNDs@Urea-1 powders, while “B” and “D” were filled with phosphorescent CNDs@Urea-2 powders. The letters “BYD” are visible under 365 nm irradiation. When the UV light is turned off, the encryption of “BYD” could be readily visualized due to “Y” with turquoise-color RTP and “BD” with yellow-color RTP, respectively. Other phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns, such as “I Love CZU” and “I Love SEU” patterns could also be simply made on the table, as shown in Fig. 7b [36]. In addition, CNDs powder is easy to absorbs moisture in the air and transforms into a gel-like material. Therefore, the CNDs gel-like material was used as the imprinting model for fingerprint tracking and recognition. The texture of the fingerprint can be obviously identified by the naked eyes under 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 7c). The results show that CNDs and CNDs@Urea have practical application value in fingerprint recognition and information encryption.

In summary, by means of simple synthesis, we have accomplished concentration controlled photochromic emitters with excellent luminescence performance. The CNDs and CNDs@Urea materials with concentration dependent and color-tunable FL and RTP, respectively. It can be confirmed that CNDs have different luminescence modes at low and high concentrations, corresponding to intrinsic-state and assembled-state, respectively. Due to the concentration dependent and tunable FL emission of CNDs, they can be applied to cell imaging. The gel-like material formed by the moisture absorption of CNDs can be used for fingerprint tracking and recognition. In addition, CNDs@Urea materials have been successfully utilized for advanced information protection. This research provides a practical and highly effective method to obtain concentration-dependent fluorescent and phosphorescent materials.

Libo Qin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yang Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuanyuan Zhang: Software, Resources, Formal analysis. Wei Gao: Software, Resources, Formal analysis. Xiaoyue Meng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yufei Bai: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kexing Geng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Pengyan Niu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yiru Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Na Wu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jianliang Bai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jianlong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
